---
title: "Oklahoma HB 2259 / Rule 8 Ability-to-Pay — Project State & Runbook"
author: "Sydney Jones Wellborn"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date())`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)  # this is a runbook; chunks document commands, not knit output
```

# Project overview

**Research question.** How did Oklahoma Rule 8 *ability-to-pay* hearing
practices change after **HB 2259 took effect on 2022-11-01**? The design
is a pre/post comparison of court docket activity and
legal-financial-obligation (LFO) events drawn from the Oklahoma State
Courts Network (OSCN).

**Data source & access.** OSCN, governed by *2009 OK 78*.
Screen-scraping is permitted at **1 request/second**; **bulk
redistribution of raw data is prohibited** (a data-retention statement
confirms no redistribution). Automated clients were previously blocked
by a Cloudflare **Turnstile** challenge (TLS-fingerprint based). This is
now resolved: the OSCN webmaster issued a **personal whitelist GUID**,
sent as the `User-Agent` string, which lets a plain `httr2` client
through at 1 req/sec. The GUID lives in `~/.Renviron` as `OSCN_UA_GUID`
and is never hardcoded, committed, or pasted anywhere.

**Windows.**

| Period | Range                   | Notes                             |
|--------|-------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Pre    | 2022-08-03 → 2022-10-31 | 90 calendar days before HB 2259   |
| Post   | 2022-11-01 → 2023-01-29 | 90 calendar days on/after HB 2259 |

Weekdays only (courts don't sit weekends). This 90-day pre/post pass is
a **validation / county-selection pass**, not the final dataset — the
eventual analysis expands to **2018–2025**.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Architecture & standing principles

These hold across all phases and are the reason the pipeline is
restart-safe and reproducible.

-   **Immutable, write-once chunks.** Every checkpoint writes a *new*
    parquet file (`chunk_NNNN.parquet`) via a temp-name + atomic rename.
    Existing files are never modified. This is the fix for an earlier
    silent-data-loss bug (a read-modify-write merge).
-   **Append-only manifests = resume-safety.** Each stage logs every
    unit of work to a `manifest.csv`. Reruns skip completed work. You
    can `Esc`/kill/restart R freely — at most one in-flight request is
    lost and re-fetched. **Trust the disk, not the console.**
-   **The pipeline is authoritative for provenance.** County, case type,
    case number, query date, and scrape timestamp are stamped from
    *ground truth* (the request parameters, the folder, the filename) —
    never trusted from parsed page metadata, which can be absent.
-   **Reproducibility.** Sampling uses a fixed seed **and**
    deterministic frame ordering (parquet read order isn't guaranteed),
    with the RNG kind pinned; the drawn sample is saved to CSV.
-   **Politeness / safety.** A hard ≥1s inter-request floor plus jitter;
    a **challenge tripwire** halts the run if a 403/Turnstile ever
    appears (it shouldn't, with the whitelist).
-   **Machine-independent config.** `PROJECT_ROOT` is resolved with
    `here::here()`; the only per-machine setting is `OSCN_UA_GUID` in
    `~/.Renviron`. Config is safe to commit.
-   **Auto-run guards.** Worker files (`fetch_details.R`,
    `parse_details.R`, `classify_lfo.R`) auto-run only when they're the
    top-level `Rscript` job *and* not suppressed
    (`.oscn_should_autorun()`); wrapper scripts set
    `options(oscn.autorun.suppress = TRUE)` before sourcing them and
    guard their **own** entry point with plain `!interactive()`.

> **Two files named `config.R`.** `scripts/phase1/config.R`
> (credentials, counties, CaseTypeIDs, windows, paths) and
> `scripts/phase2/config.R` (detail-fetch paths; sources phase 1's).
> **Do not swap them** — this has bitten us. Paths in this doc are
> always given in full.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Repository layout

```         
scripts/
  parser.R                         # validated daily-docket listing parser (parse_oscn_page)
  phase1/
    config.R                       # GUID, 13 counties, CaseTypeIDs (CF=31,CM=32,TR=18), windows, paths
    http.R                         # rate-limited httr2 GET + challenge tripwire (oscn_get, is_challenge)
    pipeline.R                     # build_url, fetch_and_parse, make_combos, run_combos, provenance
    run_smoke.R                    # small validation slice
    run_phase1.R                   # full 13-county x CF/CM/TR x pre/post pull
    verify_counties.R              # 1 request/county to confirm db codes resolve
    screening_report.R             # OPTION A: pre/post volume table by county
  phase2/
    config.R                       # DETAIL_ROOT, DETAIL_LOG, PHASE1_CHUNK_ROOT (sources phase1/config.R)
    build_queue.R                  # unique (county, case_type, case_number) work queue
    fetch_details.R                # STAGE 1: fetch GetCaseInformation pages -> raw .html.gz
    parse_case_detail.R            # STAGE 2 parser: one page -> tidy docket rows + metadata
    parse_details.R                # STAGE 2 driver: all pages -> data/case_docket chunks
    lfo_codes.csv                  # the (county, code) LFO dictionary
    classify_lfo.R                 # STAGE 3: tag rows vs dictionary, coverage, per-case summary
    build_dictionary_todo.R        # scaffold unmapped codes -> lfo_codes_TODO.csv
    merge_dictionary.R             # append filled TODO rows into lfo_codes.csv (dedup, backup)
    sample_counties.R              # OPTION B: balanced sample frame/draw + PI markdown summary
    run_sample.R                   # OPTION B end-to-end, seeded & reproducible
    status.R                       # fetched-vs-parsed per county (diagnostics)
data/
  pilot_chunks/{county}/{case_type}/chunk_NNNN.parquet     # Phase 1 docket listings
  case_details/{county}/{case_type}/{case}.html.gz          # Phase 2 raw detail pages
  case_docket/{county}/{case_type}/chunk_NNNN.parquet       # Phase 2 parsed dockets
  case_docket_classified/                                    # Phase 2 classified (regenerable)
logs/
  phase1/manifest.csv              # Phase 1 combos
  phase2/manifest.csv              # Phase 2 detail-fetch manifest
  phase2_parse/manifest.csv        # Phase 2 parse manifest
  sample_draw.{rds,csv}            # the reproducible Option B draw
reports/
  county_screening.md              # PI-facing screening summary
```

> `data/` and `.Renviron` must be **git-ignored** — raw OSCN data cannot
> be redistributed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Phase 1 — Daily docket collection

**Goal.** For each county × case type × weekday, pull the day's docket
listing and record which cases had activity that day. Cheap: **1 request
per county × case-type × day**, regardless of county size.

**Mechanics.** URL is the `WebJudicialDocketCaseTypeAll` report:
`report.asp?...&db=<county>&CaseTypeID=<id>&StartDate=<YYYY-MM-DD>...`,
where `CaseTypeID` is numeric (**CF=31, CM=32, TR=18**, confirmed
empirically). A `StartDate` returns every case with a docket *event* on
that date, regardless of filing year — so **one row = one case-day**,
not one case (a case active on 5 days appears 5 times).

**Scope.** 13 OCIS counties:
`adair, canadian, cleveland, comanche, ellis, garfield, logan, oklahoma, payne, pushmataha, rogermills, rogers, tulsa`.

**Run.**

```{bash phase1-run}
# 0. one-time: confirm all 13 db codes resolve (esp. rogermills)
Rscript scripts/phase1/verify_counties.R

# 1. full pull (resume-safe; skips anything already in the manifest)
caffeinate -i Rscript scripts/phase1/run_phase1.R
```

**Output.** `data/pilot_chunks/{county}/{case_type}/chunk_NNNN.parquet`
(17 data columns + stamped provenance:
`county, case_type, case_type_id, query_date, scraped_at, scrape_run`)
and `logs/phase1/manifest.csv` (one row per combo, with `status` = ok /
empty / error / parse_error). `empty`/zero-row days cluster on court
holidays — expected, not missing data.

**State.** The original **4-county** pull validated end-to-end (\~69k
docket rows). The config is now expanded to **all 13 counties** for the
screening pass (§Phase 3). Extending the run only fetches the 9 new
counties — the manifest skips the original 4.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Phase 2 — Case-detail collection, parsing & LFO classification

**Guiding decision (standing).** *Pull broad once, filter locally.* The
daily-docket listing text is inconsistent across counties, so LFO/Rule 8
relevance **cannot** be filtered at the listing level. We therefore
fetch the full detail page for **every** case, store it, and classify
locally — and re-classify (never re-fetch) as the dictionary evolves.

## Stage 1 — Fetch detail pages (`fetch_details.R`)

Fetches `GetCaseInformation.aspx?db=<county>&number=<case_number>` for
each unique `(county, case_type, case_number)` from Phase 1, and stores
the **raw HTML** write-once as
`data/case_details/{county}/{case_type}/{case}.html.gz`. Rate-limited,
resume-safe (`logs/phase2/manifest.csv`). Storing raw HTML means the
expensive fetch happens **once** even if the parser changes later.

```{bash phase2-fetch}
caffeinate -i Rscript scripts/phase2/fetch_details.R   # ~1/sec; the full 31k pull is ~8-9 hrs
```

## Stage 2 — Parse (`parse_case_detail.R` + `parse_details.R`)

`parse_case_detail()` turns one page into tidy docket rows: from the
embedded `<script id="json_style">` JSON (case metadata) and the
`table.docketlist` (six columns: **date, code, description, count,
party, amount**; `addtl-text` rows are merged into the entry above).
**Amounts are signed** — `$255.00` → `255`. `parse_details.R` maps this
over all stored pages into `data/case_docket/...` chunks and **stamps
county/case_type/case_number from the folder+filename** (authoritative —
the JSON can be absent on some pages).

```{bash phase2-parse}
Rscript scripts/phase2/parse_details.R
Rscript scripts/phase2/status.R          # fetched-vs-parsed per county; any gap = failures to inspect
```

## Stage 3 — Classify (`classify_lfo.R` + `lfo_codes.csv`)

Tags each docket row against the **`(county, code)`-keyed** dictionary
and writes row-level classified data to `data/case_docket_classified/`
(regenerable — safe to overwrite). It is **outcome-agnostic**: it does
*not* pick a single outcome. From the tagged rows, all four quantities
are computable (see `summarise_cases()`):

-   **`total_debits`** — sum of positive amounts (LFOs *assessed*),
    excluding conversion artifacts
-   **`total_credits`** — sum of negative amounts (waivers / credits /
    time-served)
-   **`net_balance`** — net of the above
-   **`n_rule8_events`** — count of Rule 8 / ability-to-pay events

**Two data-integrity notes baked in:**

1.  **Signed ledger.** The docket is a running account: debits
    (assessments) *and* credits (`CTS` credit-time-served, `CFOWAV`
    waiver, etc., negative). "Assessed", "waived", and "net" are three
    different sums — never blindly sum `amount`.
2.  **Mainframe-conversion artifacts.** `AC##` codes dated `1999-10-11`
    are lump balances dumped at digitization, not real-time assessments.
    They're flagged `is_conversion_artifact` and quarantined so they
    don't corrupt event-timed pre/post comparisons. (Note: a few `AC##`
    codes are *real* current fees — mapped individually, not by prefix.)

**Rule 8 detection.** Code-based (`RULE8`, `BWIR8`, `NOHM`, `DUNLT`) for
the confirmatory analysis; a **text fallback** (`"RULE 8"` /
`"ABILITY TO PAY"`) is used for cross-county screening so it works
before the dictionary is complete.

**State.** **2,723 cases parsed across all 13 counties, zero parse
errors.** The dictionary is currently **Cleveland-seeded (\~48 codes)**;
the other counties' codes are correctly *keyed* but mostly `UNMAPPED`
until the TODO is filled (next section). Amounts are captured regardless
of mapping; **`total_debits`/`total_credits` are not trustworthy until
`unmapped_codes()` is near zero for the counties of interest.**

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# LFO code dictionary — the manual workflow (do this from here)

The dictionary (`scripts/phase2/lfo_codes.csv`) maps each
`(county, code)` to a `category`, `direction` (debit/credit/neutral),
`is_rule8`, and `is_conversion_artifact`. Building it is the one
irreducibly **manual** step (it's a taxonomy judgment). The loop is
three scripts + one edit, fully reproducible:

### Step 1 — Scaffold the unmapped codes

```{bash dict-step1}
Rscript scripts/phase2/build_dictionary_todo.R
```

Writes `scripts/phase2/lfo_codes_TODO.csv`: every money-bearing code
**not** yet in the dictionary, **per county**, sorted by dollar total,
pre-filled with `n_rows`, `total_amount`, and its own description in
`notes`.

### Step 2 — Fill it in (manual)

Open `scripts/phase2/lfo_codes_TODO.csv` and, for each row, fill:

-   **`category`** — e.g. `court_cost`, `fee`, `admin_fee`, `fine`,
    `state_assessment`, `jail_cost`, `warrant`,
    `rule8_hearing`/`rule8_enforcement`, `credit`, `waiver`,
    `collection_fee`, `bond`, `conversion_artifact`, or
    `noise`/`exclude` for ledger junk (e.g. "LUNCH FOR JURY").
-   **`direction`** — `debit` (assessment, positive), `credit`
    (waiver/payment, negative), or `neutral` (informational/fund
    allocation).
-   **`is_rule8`** — `TRUE` for ability-to-pay / Rule 8 codes.
-   **`is_conversion_artifact`** — `TRUE` only for genuine
    mainframe-conversion balances.

Work biggest-dollar-first (rows are pre-sorted); the top \~30 codes
usually carry 90%+ of the money. **Spot-check descriptions across
counties** before copying a category — most codes are statewide, but
per-county keying exists precisely because some (e.g. cost-hearing
terminology, CCH vs RCC) diverge.

> Rows left with a **blank `category` are skipped** by the merge (they
> stay `UNMAPPED` and resurface next time), so you can't half-map a
> code.

### Step 3 — Merge into the dictionary

```{bash dict-step3}
Rscript scripts/phase2/merge_dictionary.R
```

Appends filled rows into `lfo_codes.csv`, **refusing duplicate
`(county, code)` pairs** and **backing up** to `lfo_codes.csv.bak`
first.

### Step 4 — Re-classify (no re-fetch, no re-parse)

```{bash dict-step4}
Rscript scripts/phase2/classify_lfo.R
```

Regenerates `data/case_docket_classified/` and prints remaining unmapped
codes. **Repeat Steps 1–4** until `unmapped_codes()` is at (or near)
zero for the counties you care about — or a deliberate ignore-set.

> **Sampling caveat.** The current TODO is built from a
> **150-case-per-county sample**, so it covers the *common/high-dollar*
> codes but will miss rare ones. When you later pull *full* detail for
> the chosen counties, **re-run Steps 1–4** — expect a fresh tail.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Phase 3 — County screening & selection (the PI decision)

To choose which counties to study in depth, we screen **all 13**
cheaply, then deep-pull only the winners. Two options were prepared;
**the sample for both has already been drawn, fetched, and parsed**
(seed `20221101`, 75 pre + 75 post per county, 1,806 case-strata;
`logs/sample_draw.csv` is the reproducible record).

## Option A — Structural screen (volume / coverage)

For a design like **2 rural + 2 urban**: which counties have enough
clean pre/post case volume?

```{bash optA}
Rscript scripts/phase1/screening_report.R   # pre/post unique-case table by county & case type
```

Answers "where is there adequate data." Does **not** speak to
ability-to-pay frequency. (Note: Roger Mills returned only 3 sampled
cases — likely genuinely too thin to study.)

## Option B — Treatment-relevance screen (Rule 8 / LFO activity) *(PI likely choice)*

Which counties actually show HB 2259-relevant activity? Uses the
balanced sample to estimate per-county Rule 8 and LFO **rates**, pre vs
post.

```{bash optB}
# end-to-end, reproducible: draw (seeded) -> fetch -> parse -> classify -> PI summary
caffeinate -i Rscript scripts/phase2/run_sample.R
# regenerate just the report after a dictionary update:
# Rscript -e 'source("scripts/phase2/sample_counties.R"); write_pi_summary(readRDS("logs/sample_draw.rds"))'
```

Produces **`reports/county_screening.md`** with both tables for the PI.

**How to read the screen (important):**

-   **`LFO pre/post`** = share of sampled cases with **any** positive
    amount assessed in that window (incidence, not disposition, not
    severity). **`Rule 8 pre/post`** = share with any ability-to-pay
    activity in that window.
-   **Balanced 50/50 sampling measures rate/composition, not volume.**
    Volume change comes from Option A's counts. (Rule 8 *volume* ≈
    sampled rate × true period case-count.)
-   **Screening only.** These rates guide selection; they are **not** an
    effect estimate — a case active across the boundary contributes to
    both columns, and cases were sampled by period, so the pre/post
    columns are not independent groups.

> For Option B to be trustworthy, **complete the LFO dictionary on the
> 13-county sample first** (the manual workflow above) so `lfo_rate`
> isn't undercounting on unmapped codes.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# What happens next — by PI decision

## If the PI chooses Option B (treatment-relevance — expected)

1.  **Finish the dictionary on the 13-county sample** (LFO manual
    workflow, Steps 1–4) so the rates in `county_screening.md` are
    reliable.
2.  **Regenerate** `county_screening.md`; PI picks the \~4 counties by
    Rule 8 / LFO activity.
3.  **Full detail pull for the chosen counties.** The sampled pages are
    already on disk and will be skipped; fetch the *remaining* cases for
    those counties (the full `(county, case_number)` set from Phase 1,
    not just the sample). The original 4-county detail fetch was **never
    completed** (\~986 + sample only), so this is a real, `caffeinate`d,
    multi-hour, resume-safe run.
4.  **Re-parse, re-run the dictionary loop** for the full data (expect a
    new code tail), classify.
5.  **Confirmatory analysis** — full data at *natural* volumes,
    **code-based** classification, proper event-timed pre/post
    comparison of assessed / waived / net / Rule 8 counts.

## If the PI chooses Option A (structural — 2 rural + 2 urban)

1.  Pick counties from `screening_report.R` against a minimum-N floor
    (mind that Oklahoma/Tulsa dwarf the rurals — set a floor, don't rank
    raw).
2.  **Full detail pull** for the chosen 4 (sampled pages skipped), then
    dictionary loop → classify.
3.  Confirmatory analysis as above. (Option A skips the sampled-rate
    step but the downstream is identical.)

## Either way — scaling to the full study

The pre/post pass is validation. To go to the **full 2018–2025** scope,
the *only* substantive change is the **date grid** (`build_date_grid()`
in `scripts/phase1/config.R`) — everything else (pipeline, chunk writer,
manifests, throttle, parsers) is already year-agnostic and resume-safe.
Re-run Phase 1 then Phase 2 for the chosen counties over the full span.
(At \~10k files/county/case type over 8 years, consider compacting
finished years into partitioned datasets later — the write-once design
makes that safe.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Gotchas & lessons log

-   **Trust the disk, not the console.** Manifests + immutable chunks
    mean a spinner, "suspended" session, or `Esc` never costs data.
    Re-run to resume.
-   **Auto-run guards.** Sourcing a worker file used to launch its full
    run. Fixed: workers use `.oscn_should_autorun()` (suppressible);
    wrappers use `!interactive()`. If a script "does nothing" when
    `Rscript`-ed, check it isn't suppressing its own entry point.
-   **Config drift across machines.** `CASE_TYPE_IDS` and `PROJECT_ROOT`
    are now baked/derived; only `OSCN_UA_GUID` in `~/.Renviron` is
    per-machine. `.Renviron` is read once at R startup — **restart R
    after editing it.**
-   **Provenance from ground truth.** NA counties came from trusting
    page metadata; the driver now stamps county/case_type/case_number
    from the folder+filename.
-   **Pick one scrape machine** so the fetch manifest has a single
    source of truth (currently the desktop). `caffeinate -i` keeps it
    awake; the lid closing still sleeps on battery.
-   **Two `config.R` files** — never swap phase 1's for phase 2's.
-   **iCloud Drive** on `Documents/` can sync/alter files across
    machines; pause/verify before reorganizing.

# Immediate next actions

1.  `Rscript scripts/phase1/verify_counties.R` — confirm `rogermills`
    (and settle whether it's too small to keep).
2.  Complete the **LFO dictionary** on the 13-county sample (manual
    workflow, Steps 1–4) until `unmapped_codes()` is \~empty for the
    counties of interest.
3.  `Rscript scripts/phase2/run_sample.R` (or just regenerate the
    report) → hand the PI **`reports/county_screening.md`** with both
    options.
4.  On the PI's county choice → **full detail pull** for the chosen
    counties → dictionary loop → classify → confirmatory analysis. Then
    expand to 2018–2025 via the date grid.
